data.title: The $500,000 Portfolio To Potentially Pay All Your Bills
data.description:
Introduction:

Everyone's situation is unique, and one size never fits all. However, there can be broadly defined ideas that may suit a large number of investors planning for retirement. It is always good to learn from different ideas and strategies. In that spirit, we are going to provide a retirement strategy that can potentially pay your bills and spending needs without much fuss. However, we also have to make sure that the strategy ensures that it accounts for the inflation and growth of the portfolio to cover a long retirement.

Over the last several decades, the average life expectancy has grown consistently, not only in the U.S. but for most of the world. Though in recent years, it has shown signs of stagnating growth. Nonetheless, the idea is that today's retirees should plan for at least 30-35 years of retirement. So, their investment strategies should suitably account for this longevity. Most corporations no longer provide any kind of fixed pensions, and most retirees have to rely upon their own savings. However, Social Security and Medicare still remain two important pillars to support retirement years for the vast majority of retirees.

Now, how much should you save? There is no one good answer. It depends on your own spending needs and your living standards prior to retirement. According to a study by Fidelity, one should aim for 8 to 10 times one's last salary (or gross income), just before retirement. This assumes that you would want to be able to afford a similar living standard as you were used to prior to retirement. Sure, there are going to be many changes in your lifestyle and day-to-day routine once you retire. There will be many expenses that will no longer be needed, but at the same time, there may be some additional expenses. The place or the state where you choose to retire may have a significant impact on your expense budget. There are many budget-friendly countries outside the U.S. where the same dollar can go much farther, but that may not suit everyone. So, all these considerations should be given thought.

Based on the above, we think we can draw some broad guidelines as to what one should plan for at a minimum. This is just to provide a very broad picture; however, your own numbers could be different based on your unique situation.

Your annual gross income (for a couple) in the last two years prior to retirement

Minimum savings (at retirement)

Desirable level of savings (at retirement)

< $75,000

$500,000

$750,000

Between $75,000 and $100,000

$800,000

$1.2 million

Between $100,000 and $150,000

$1.2 million

$1.5 million

Between $150,000 and $200,000

$1.5 million

$2.0 million

And so on.

Please note that the above savings exclude your primary home, where you are going to live in retirement.

Keeping these numbers, we are going to provide two portfolios, one with investment capital of $500,000, and another with $1.0 million.

Investment Thesis:

If you are a very active investor and enjoy reading & researching stocks/markets every single day, the portfolios described in this article are not for you. These portfolios are for folks who are not very active investors, but still want to manage their own portfolios.

Our portfolios will primarily focus on the following aspects:

  1. The portfolio should provide enough income to meet our day-to-day expenses comfortably. For this, what if we invest in companies (and brands) that meet our daily needs? First, most folks are aware of what companies provide those products and services they like and use on a day-to-day basis. For example, we all use cell phone services and internet providers. There is a limited number of providers in the market, and we also get to know the companies that are efficient and doing a good job in providing those services. If we were to invest in those companies, we could use their dividend income to meet our monthly expenses on those services.
  2. Secondly, we should be able to count on these companies to provide sufficient dividend growth to meet any inflation in the cost of their services. This will not always match, but broadly it should match over a long time frame. In addition, we should expect that there will be some share price appreciation to provide us further cover against inflation and provide longevity to our portfolio. We should also pay attention to diversification, which is always important.
  3. It may not be sufficient to invest in just dividend-paying companies; a small portion of our portfolio should be invested in high-growth companies to provide the boost in growth over time.

To implement this strategy, we will first list all the major and common budget items that we spend our money on. However, we will focus on the necessary spending items and lump the rest as one big budget item as "discretionary spending". That said, we will still list some of the big-ticket items, such as housing costs and healthcare. Here are our most basic spending needs items:

  • Housing: It does not matter if you own the house or rent it, but you need a place to live, and there is a cost to it. However, most retirees would rather own a house than rent it. However, even if you own the house outright (fully paid basis), there will be expenses on property taxes, insurance, HOA membership, and general upkeep. It is always preferable to pay off any mortgage you may have prior to retirement.
  • Foods & Groceries: This is again the most basic one and should include your monthly expenses on food and groceries, including household need items. However, this should not include eating out in restaurants or fast food.
  • Utilities: This should cover the basic utilities such as electricity, gas, and water.
  • Communication services: This should include internet, cell-phone services, and streaming services, if any.
  • Healthcare: This is a tricky one and can vary a lot based on your personal situation, especially the age at which you retire. If you retire prior to reaching the age of 65, your healthcare costs could be higher. However, they are more predictable at or after 65 when you are eligible for Medicare.
  • Eating out expenses: Any expenses on eating out at restaurants on a monthly (average) basis, including fast food.
  • Transportation: The need for transportation goes down in retirement, as you would not be commuting to the workplace. However, there is not much public transportation in the U.S., so there will be a need for at least one car (if not two), which will also require upkeep and gas expenses.
  • Discretionary Spending: This is the last budget item and would include anything and everything that is not covered previously. This also provides a lot of discretionary power to decide what to spend on and how much to spend.

Once we have defined the spending heads, we need to estimate the dollar amount under each budget head. Following that, we will proportionately invest our capital in stocks and securities of businesses that meet those needs. Our goal is first to meet the income needs, and then the rest of the capital can be invested for growth. This is where there will be more flexibility with investors who have a larger pool of capital.

We will now provide two tables, the first for folks with $500,000 in savings, and the second for folks with $1 million in savings. These tables also provide estimated expenses under each budget head for two categories. Please note your own expenses will most definitely vary, with some items being more while others being less.

Table-1: Monthly budget for a couple based on spending heads

Please see the details of available funds in the tables under the next section (table-1A and 1B).

Investment Portfolio:

In this strategy, we will invest in companies that provide the specific goods or services under each of the above budget heads. However, some stocks or securities may not match exactly with the budget item. Also, this is not exactly a growth portfolio, so this may suit only retirees who happen to prioritize regular income over growth. Since discretionary spending does not fall under any specific budget head, we will invest in general funds to provide the income and growth. Please note that some budget heads are showing a deficit in relation to the income generated, but that is compensated by excess income in some other budget heads or from the social security income.

The major funds (CEFs, ETFs) or individual stocks are listed here:

Funds: (PDI), (HOMZ), (UTG), (UTF), (BME), (HQL), (AMLP), (PEO), (ADX), and (JEPQ).

Stocks: (MAA), (EQR), (AMH), (TSN), (PG), (NSRGY), (CLX), (PEP), (ATO), (VZ), (TU), (QSR), and (MCD).

Table-1A: Details of spending versus income for a $500,000 portfolio

Table-1B: Details of spending versus income for a $1 Million portfolio

In both portfolios ($500K and $1 Million), we have included a budget item as "discretionary" with a large monthly allocation to cover any expenses that may have been skipped elsewhere. In addition to that, there is some surplus income left at the end of each of the portfolios that the investor/retiree could spend on exigencies or invest back into their portfolio.

Discussion about Stocks/Funds used in the portfolio:

Housing:

In the housing category, we are selecting two funds and three individual stocks.

The two funds are: PDI and HOMZ.

Our first fund, PDI, does not correspond to the housing sector directly, but it does provide a very high income and a somewhat indirect relationship to the sector. It is a multi-sector fund from the Pimco group and invests roughly 25% of its assets in privately issued (non-agency) mortgage securities and another 25% in high-yield credit securities. It provides a reliable and high amount of monthly distribution.

HOMZ is an ETF from Hoya Capital Group, focused on many segments of the housing sector. Its top holdings include Lowe's (LOW), Home Depot (HD), Toll Brothers (TOL), and Pulte (PHM). The yield is only about 2.6%, but it will add to the diversification.

The three stocks are MAA, EQR, and AMH:

The first two stocks represent REIT companies that are in the business of owning, developing, and renting multifamily apartment communities. MAA primarily operates in the Sunbelt area (southeastern and southwestern) of the U.S., while EQR is concentrated in the Northeast, D.C., and the West Coast.

AMH (American Homes 4 Rent) is again a REIT and engaged in owning, operating, and renting/leasing single-family homes throughout the U.S., but primarily in southern and midwestern regions of the U.S. It also owns a large number of land holdings. Its main source of income is from the rental revenue from single-family homes.

Utilities:

We have selected one stock and two funds. The first company, Atmos Energy (ATO), is a regulated natural gas utility company with a very stable business. However, the yield is towards the lower end, roughly 2.2%.

We are also selecting two funds, namely, UTG (from Reaves) and UTF from the Cohen & Steers group. Both are excellent funds, but a bit expensive right now, as utility companies have appreciated in the last year due to the AI narrative. Both funds have proven records of performance for the last multiple decades, but some of the holdings may overlap between the two funds.

Food and Household:

Under this category, we have selected five consumer staple companies, and all of them lead in their respective segments. They also provide a decent level of dividend income ranging between 2.5% to 4.5%. These five companies are Tyson Foods (TSN), Procter & Gamble (PG), Nestlé (NSRGY), Clorox Co. (CLX), and PepsiCo (PEP).

Healthcare:

We have included two CEFs under this sector, BlackRock Health Sciences (BME) and Abrdn Life Sciences Investors Fund (HQL).

For folks who have a larger pool of capital and thus can manage on less income, they could replace HQL or BME with Janus Henderson Global Life Sciences Fund (JFNIX). This mutual fund will provide an income yield in the range of 5% (mostly towards the end of the year), but has a much better performance record in terms of price appreciation.

Both BME and JFNIX invest in stocks of companies operating across all four major segments of the healthcare sector, namely, healthcare equipment and services, pharmaceuticals, biotechnology, and life sciences. However, HQL is heavily concentrated in biotechnology, with over 70% of the assets.

Communications:

There are three major communications companies in the U.S., namely AT&T (T), Verizon (VZ), and T-Mobile (TMUS). If your capital base is large enough, we would recommend allocating equal amounts to all three. Please note that T-Mobile provides a much lower yield of under 2% but is growing at a faster rate. However, if you are on a smaller capital base and still need to drive higher income, simply invest in Verizon and AT&T. You may even derive higher income from Canadian communication companies such as TELUS Corp. (TU), which pays a roughly 8.9% yield.

Dining & Entertainment:

We are including two companies from the restaurant business.

McDonald's Corp. (MCD) is the leading fast-food chain company in the U.S. and overseas as well. The dividend yield is somewhat low at 2.25%, but it is hard to find a time when this company trades cheaper, except during major panic situations. However, besides dividends, this company has a proven record of providing excellent appreciation over the years.

We can compensate for the lower yield of MCD (roughly 2.25%) with the higher yield (> 3.5%) from QSR (Restaurant Brands International). It is a global restaurant chain business with over 30,000 locations worldwide. Nearly half of the outlets are outside the U.S. It owns or operates restaurant brands and retail chains such as Burger King, Tim Hortons, Popeyes Louisiana Kitchen, and Firehouse Subs.

Transportation:

Your transportation costs can vary greatly, depending on your lifestyle and how much you travel. But, generally speaking, it should be much less than what it would be prior to retirement.

We are including the midstream MLPs-based ETF, namely, Alerian (AMLP), along with one closed-end fund, Adams Natural Resources (PEO). AMLP will give you exposure to all the major midstream MLPs, while PEO will provide exposure to the major oil companies (that too at a discount). Both PEO and MLPX provide reasonably high yields, roughly in the 7.5% range.

Misc Category:

This category is kept to cover discretionary spending or items that are not included in any other category.

As you can see in the tables above, we have kept a large amount for this category (undefined expense items).

For this category, we will keep two funds that also provide high income and a decent shot at capital appreciation over time.

ADX is one of the best CEFs out there for dividend investors, providing 2% of its average NAV on a quarterly basis, which translates to roughly 8% yield, but it can vary. The fund has a very long and impressive history of performance as well. I

We are going to select a second fund that invests in high-growth companies. It should preferably also use a call options strategy so that it provides high income as well. There are several good names in this category, such as Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Premium Income ETF (GPIQ), JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ), and Eaton Vance Enhanced Equity Income Fund II (EOS).

Likely Risks with the Portfolio:

Investors should be aware of potential risks with the portfolios suggested above.

Market risk: All investments in the suggested portfolios are subject to market risk. Unexpected situations may arise as the future is always unpredictable, and the portfolio may not perform as expected.

Valuation risk: Even though the market has been somewhat down in the last few days (or weeks), it still may be overvalued. Investors should pay attention to the valuation of each fund or stock before buying.

No downward protection: Suggested portfolios have no downward protection or hedging. During an economic downturn or some panic correction, it can have drawdowns similar to the broader market. Also, retirees should pay attention to the 'sequencing risk' (risk of the market having a deep and sustained correction during the initial couple of years of retirement).

Sensitivity to Interest Rates: Some (if not most) of the stocks presented in this article are sensitive to high interest rates. If the rates were to stay high (or rise) due to a rise in inflation (or otherwise), they would be impacted negatively.

Leverage: There are a few securities in the suggested portfolios that use leverage, though overall leverage is minimal. Leverage can make a security more volatile and can add to the risk.

Concluding Thoughts:

We have presented two similar portfolios in this article, catering to investors with $500,000 to $1 million in savings capital. The portfolio presented has a total of 23 stocks/securities, with 9 funds and 14 individual stocks. Out of 9 funds, there are 5 CEFs and 4 ETFs. In addition to providing all the needed income, it provides market-matching performance over a long time horizon. In our backtesting, after making some suitable changes (as not all securities have long enough histories), starting Jan. 2006 until Feb.2026, both portfolios would have provided over 11.2% (annualized), compared to 10.8% from the S&P 500. However, during the last 10 years, the S&P 500 outperformed almost anything, and these portfolios are no exception.

Also, the overall portfolios are quite diversified into many sectors and segments of the economy. This is natural for these portfolios, as they are structured based on the type of spending budget heads.

Analyst's Disclosure: I/we have a beneficial long position in the shares of ABT, ABBV, CI, JNJ, PFE, NVS, NVO, AZN, UNH, CL, CLX, UL, NSRGY, PG, TSN, ADM, BTI, MO, PM, KO, PEP, EXC, D, DEA, DEO, ENB, MCD, BAC, PRU, UPS, WMT, WBA, CVS, LOW, AAPL, IBM, CSCO, MSFT, INTC, T, VZ, CVX, XOM, VLO, ABB, ITW, MMM, LMT, LYB, RIO, O, NNN, WPC, ARCC, ARDC, AWF, BME, BST, CHI, DNP, USA, UTF, UTG, RFI, RNP, RQI, EVT, EOS, FFC, GOF, HQH, HTA, IFN, HYB, JPC, JPS, TLT either through stock ownership, options, or other derivatives. I wrote this article myself, and it expresses my own opinions. I am not receiving compensation for it (other than from Seeking Alpha). I have no business relationship with any company whose stock is mentioned in this article.

Disclaimer: The information presented in this article is for informational purposes only and in no way should be construed as financial advice or a recommendation to buy or sell any stock. The author is not a financial advisor. Please always do further research and do your own due diligence before making any investments. Every effort has been made to present the data/information accurately; however, the author does not claim 100% accuracy. The stock portfolios presented here are model portfolios for demonstration purposes. For the complete list of our LONG positions, please see our profile on Seeking Alpha.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Globe Life: Undervalued Insurance For Your Portfolio
data.description:
Co-authored by Kody's Dividends

When investors are building a dividend portfolio, they're often forced to balance their current need for yield today and their desire for dividend growth tomorrow. The logic goes that the younger you are, the lower yield you can accept because you have more time for that growth runway to benefit you. Unfortunately, what this causes is many investors to think that they need a higher yield than they actually need to be sustainable in the long run. They overshoot their yield and end up falling into a realm of dividend cuts and unsustainable dividends or enter a realm where the performance of their portfolio is solely governed by the dividend yield of their portfolio, and their capital either erodes or sits unmoving in value as inflation slowly eats it up.

For many, they would simply say, "I could just reinvest dividends. That's all I need to do to have my portfolio value climb and my income grow." This is true. If you have a 10% yield and you're reinvesting 25%, you're only going to see a pitiful 2.5% dividend increase. The math is simple: you collect $100. You reinvest $25. You end up with $2.50 more next year.

This is the major difference between how we invest within Dividend Kings and how many dividend investors approach their portfolio. We have a combination approach, collecting high yields today from our fixed-income positions that never make it to the public and enjoying strongly growing dividends and total returns from our common equity positions that make up our portfolio. Together, we enjoy yields in the 6% to 8% range on average across the entire portfolio while enjoying dividend growth well beyond 2.5%. There's no need to climb up to 10% and reinvest 25% and get returns as far as dividend growth and capital turns that are worse than staying at a lower, more sustainable level.

Today, I want to discuss a company whose yield, for many, is going to be entirely unattractive until you recognize that it can play an important part in boosting the overall dividend growth of your portfolio.

Let's dive in!

Scoop Up This Undervalued Insurance Titan Now

Globe Life (GL) mostly provides life and supplemental health insurance policies to lower-middle-income and middle-income consumers. They reach these consumers through a combination of exclusive agents, independent agents, and direct-to-consumer channels.

As of Dec. 31, 2025, the company had 17.2 million policies in force. It generated a supermajority of its premium income from the Life segment (69% of LTM premium income). The remaining 31% was derived from the Health segment.

A major plus for GL is its business model. The company's policies are economical for the customers that it targets, ranging from $30 to $60 a month. That explains how over 90% of GL's premium revenue comes from policies sold in previous years, which is a testament to its long-term revenue visibility.

Since these policies are affordable and sticky, the company can also raise them gradually over time with little to no pushback from policyholders. This acts as an organic growth tailwind for GL.

The company also has a large addressable market. Over 100 million Americans are either uninsured or underinsured. By removing the primary barrier to entry for many of their core products (medical exam), simplifying underwriting, and using their $1 for the first month high-volume lead generation tactic, GL is doing what it can to attract more potential policyholders.

The company also has a goal to surpass 28,000 exclusive agents (up from about 17,500 currently). Since these agents are trained to work in the worksite and living room markets, they reach the underinsured directly at their place of employment or in their neighborhoods.

Finally, GL throws off enough cash flow to repurchase tons of shares. In 2025 alone, the company repurchased 5.4 million shares (down nearly 5% from 2024).

That's why we believe the FAST Graphs analyst consensus for high single-digit percentage annual diluted EPS growth is attainable. GL's A S&P credit rating also implies the company is rock-solid financially.

Its 0.8% yield is modest. Still, the payout ratio percentage is only going to be in the high single digits for 2026. This affords it the ability to hike the dividend at a low double-digit rate each year (like the 12.5% boost in 2025). That's an attractive Chowder number for a company with a 20-year dividend growth streak.

While GL is our least undervalued pick for the next week (in relative terms), it's still a solid value. At the current $140.99 share price, the stock is priced at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 9.6x. According to data from FAST Graphs, this is under the 10-year average P/E ratio of 13.5 and the 20-year average P/E ratio of 12.1.

Looking ahead, we're assigning a fair value multiple of 11. This would be about one standard deviation below the 20-year average P/E ratio and about two less than the 10-year average P/E ratio. While GL's 7.6% annual diluted EPS growth consensus is still healthy, its increased size and scale put this a bit below the 20-year average CAGR of 10.3%.

That works out to a 17% discount to our $171 fair value per share estimate. If GL meets growth forecasts and reverts to fair value, it could produce a 23% total return through March 2027.

Material risks to the company include insurance risk and events that damage its brand image.

As an insurer, GL operates in a competitive environment. This means it must balance prudent underwriting with competing on price. If it prices policies inadequately, underwriting profits could suffer. If it prices policies excessively, it could lose share to rivals. So, the company must maintain a happy medium between the two for optimal results. A failure to do so could weigh on its fundamentals.

Another risk to GL is that the sensitive information of its policyholders makes it a high-value target for hackers. If any attempted cyber breaches are widely successful, that could compromise this sensitive information. Resulting lawsuits and regulatory penalties could lead policyholders to go elsewhere, as well as cause prospective policyholders to pick competitors.

Conclusion

GL trades at an attractive discount to provide us with strong total returns and enjoy massive dividend growth. It can play an essential role in your personal dividend portfolio, not so much as a yield booster today, but the ability to provide you with a bastion of wonderful dividends tomorrow.

Investors struggle with the same struggles that everyday people struggle with because we are everyday people. A big struggle that we have as Westerners is a struggle with gratification. We live in an era of Amazon (AMZN) and Walmart (WMT) where we can get our purchases within hours or even a day or two. Instant gratification is what we're seeking, and yet often when it comes to investing, the greatest rewards come with time and diligence. Delayed gratification can provide us with a bigger payoff in the end! As dividend-focused investors who demand strong total returns and have great values for our purchases, we are focused and geared toward the bigger win in the end, not the little win right here. This means having some holdings in our portfolio that provide us with wonderful yields today and some holdings in our portfolio that will provide us with bigger yields tomorrow, combined to make a holistic approach within our Fortress portfolio that provides us with safety, income, and market-beating returns.

That's what it means to be royalty: thinking ahead, enjoying the fruits of your labor, and being the Dividend King of your portfolio.

data.title: If I Were Retired Today, These 3 Income Machines Would Be My First Buys
data.description:
Introduction

As some of you may know, I was a Corporate Treasury intern in the past. That was back in 2019 when I was still figuring out what I wanted to do in life. A few months after I left to finish my master’s degree in International Business Administration (with a focus on purchasing and supply chains), my former boss retired. That’s old news and doesn’t affect your portfolio at all. However, because Henkel (the company where I interned) is a European heavyweight, he was interviewed, as he was a rather powerful treasury manager.

I’m bringing that up because I just re-read the piece. One thing stood out to me:

Treasury chief Michael Reuter has left consumer-goods company Henkel and retired at the end of September, as DerTreasurer has learned. Shortly before his departure, he and his team were able to make a splash in the capital market: Henkel issued two sterling bonds totaling the equivalent of 850 million euros, both with a negative yield. - Der Treasurer (translated)

Again, this isn’t about Henkel or about my past. This is about the last line in that paragraph, which mentioned that Henkel issued the equivalent of EUR 850 million in negative-yielding debt. Back then, people paid corporations to take their money. Sure, Henkel is an A-rated giant with terrific diversification, but it’s still “nuts” if you think about it.

And, to use a chart from 2019, it wasn’t unusual. Back then, the total amount of debt with a negative yield was $16 trillion. More than 28% of the debt tracked by the Bloomberg Barclays Global Aggregate Index had a yield of less than 0%.

Back then, it was truly the best time to own low-risk, high-quality dividend growth stocks, as investors were aggressively chasing income as if the world would end. This was obviously fully supported by ultra-low rates in developed nations.

To me, it’s a perfect example of how major capital shifts impact our portfolios. Less than two years ago, when rates spiked, the exact opposite happened. Some investors (I’m painting with a broad brush again) sold equities and went into short-term bonds that yielded more than 5%. That explains the massive surge in money market assets, as we can see below.

While I am typing this, there’s close to $8 trillion in money market funds, which is basically short-term government debt (risk-free income, so to speak).

In 2020, that number was $5 trillion. Between 2010 and 2019, it was $3 trillion on a very consistent basis, as investors were buying income in other places, as the yield on short-term debt was close to zero.

These are the capital rotations I care so much about, as they are crucial for asset management. We’re seeing the same in equities. In the first two months of this year, investors wanted cyclical value stocks (that’s my core thesis, as most readers will know) and non-U.S. equities.

As we can see below, over the past 15 years, U.S. stocks were the place to be. However, on a year-to-date basis in 2026 (that’s January-February), U.S. stocks lagged international stocks.

The war in Iran changed that.

My friend and business partner, Albert Marko, wrote on that, as he made the case that the U.S. is using the conflict to create new capital flows into the U.S., based on the realization that in times of distress, it has the safest supply chains (think of its oil supply), dollar safety, military power, and other tailwinds.

Bloomberg just confirmed that:

But the developments since the US and Israeli attacks on Iran reveal that America is still the go-to market for investors. If the US has warts, it remains the center of global innovation and home to the deepest and most liquid markets on the planet, a feature that becomes indispensable when economic shocks hit. After 14 chaotic months, we’re also seeing signs emerge of institutional resilience at the Federal Reserve and Supreme Court, an additional source of comfort. - Bloomberg

That’s a good thing, as it not only supports my thesis that America remains a terrific (if not the best) market for long-term capital allocation, but also because the market’s biggest companies are now in need of massive funding. While most hyperscalers (think of the Mag-7) have terrific balance sheets, AI spending is now forcing them to diversify these risks.

It was just reported that Amazon (AMZN) is issuing $37 billion in bonds. According to Reuters, the bond deal resulted in $126 billion worth of demand, which is a good sign for Amazon. And then there’s Alphabet (GOOGL), which raised $100 billion, including through a 100-year bond. It was observed 10x, according to Seeking Alpha.

At this point, I have to admit that my intro seems to be all over the place (and close to 1,000 words - sorry!).

However, it brings me to my main point, which is that as global markets are getting volatile, the U.S. once again defeats the argument that it’s not the go-to place for capital anymore. We also see that Americans are using it to issue debt for the AI transition.

Unfortunately, this creates a bit of an issue. As rates are potentially falling, the environment isn’t great for income anymore. And while we’re far away from a 2019 scenario where it costs money in some cases to lend money to corporations, it’s not a scenario where I want to be forced into deals like financing the AI revolution. I want no part in that, at least not through bonds.

This brings me to the question that people ask me quite frequently, which is what I would own if I were retired right now? It’s high-quality U.S. income that comes with both income and unique characteristics that add value to most income portfolios.

With all of this in mind, let’s look at three income ideas I would buy today if I were retired.

Here’s What I Would Buy

A big part of the intro was about the credit market. I have often said that I do not like bonds, as I’m an “equity guy.” I want to own a share of a company and grow with it over time, while generating income, in some cases.

Right now, that lending market is under fire. While Alphabet and Amazon are not having a hard time finding buyers, the private credit market is seeing cracks. Many asset managers and their Business Development Companies have sold off hard this year, including some of the best players like Ares Management (ARES) and Apollo Global Management (APO), which I consider the gold standard of private credit.

Fears are basically created by software disruption and some negative headlines regarding “unexpected” defaults that make people wonder how much bad debt is hidden in this industry. When adding that private credit is very cyclical, it explains why I am so careful in this industry.

However, I still would buy exposure here, as there are some great deals out there. One of them is Ares Capital Corp. (ARCC). It’s the BDC owned by Ares Management. Right now, I am actually looking to buy ARES in the months ahead (I’ll update you on my liquidity and plans soon). However, if I were retired, I would buy the higher-yielding BDC.

Ares Capital currently yields 10.4%. This dividend hasn’t been cut since the Great Financial Crisis. And, as of December 31, 2025, it’s a BDC with a superior total return compared to banks and BDC peers, as Ares has figured out how to find a great balance between risk (yield) and safety (picking the right deals).

Moreover, not only is this the biggest BDC on the market, but also a BDC with terrific fundamentals, as it has a BBB credit rating from all three major rating agencies (Fitch even has a positive outlook, which could mean a path to BBB+), and more than $6 billion in liquidity.

Its portfolio has a nonaccrual rate of 1.8% (at cost). That’s in line with prior year levels, according to the company. Moreover, it’s below its own long-term average of 2.8% and below the BDC industry average of 3.8%. At fair value, that number is just 1.2%.

The company also believes its dividend is sustainable:

We believe ARCC is in a good position to maintain its dividend despite market expectations for further declines in short-term interest rates. We generally set our dividend level based on our view of the earnings power of our company. While lower short-term rates present an earnings headwind, we believe there are multiple factors that can support our earnings and thus, our current dividend level for the foreseeable future. - ARCC 4Q25 Earnings Call

And to incorporate higher risks, ARCC is now being traded at 7% below book value.

Another stock I would buy is Agree Realty Series A (ADC.PR.A), which is Agree Realty’s preferred stock. In other words, it’s somewhat of a hybrid between a bond and a stock. Investors get exposure to Agree Realty (equity ownership), yet no voting rights and no dividend growth. What they do get is bond-like dividend payments and more safety, as preferred stock is more “senior” than common equity.

Generally speaking, I dislike capped upside, which applies to assets without dividend growth. However, the risk/reward of this preferred stock is great, as it trades at $17.19 while I am writing this. That’s substantially lower than the liquidation price of $25. That’s the price you’ll get if the company were to buy back the preferred stock.

That limited upside isn’t great for long-term growth investors, yet it’s perfect for income, as it is a premium of 45% compared to the current price. That’s the upside you’ll get before you potentially lose these shares in a buyback. Moreover, because of the low price, the monthly dividend yield is 6.2%. That’s a terrific yield, roughly 200 basis points above the 10-year government bond.

As a comparison, Agree Realty (ADC) common stock yields 3.9%. That dividend has a five-year CAGR of 1.9%. On October 14, it raised the dividend by 2.3%. If we assume that the dividend growth rate holds, investors will end up with a yield on cost of 4.9% after ten years, which is still way below the rate on the preferred stock.

It also helps that Agree has an A-rated balance sheet, a portfolio that mostly caters to ultra-safe net lease tenants, and growth opportunities in areas like sale-leaseback. Moreover, as this preferred stock is cumulative, if Agree were to run into issues leading to dividend cuts, it would have to make preferred shareholders whole before it would be allowed to pay a common dividend again.

I really like this preferred stock. And, if I were to retire today, I would buy this in a heartbeat.

The third pick is somewhat unusual, as it’s Rayonier (RYN). This company is a specialty REIT. Technically, it’s a “Land Resources REIT,” as it owns timberland that covers more than 4 million acres after merging with PotlatchDeltic.

As a result, their business model is based on three factors. They sell timber to lumber mills, where lumber for a wide range of purposes is created. Homebuilding is a major factor. This business is cyclical, as it depends on pricing and demand. It also generates high-quality revenue from strategic master-planned communities. That’s high-quality developed land for new housing communities. It’s much less volatile than selling timber.

Last but not least, they use land for value-adding opportunities like solar, carbon credits, bioenergy, and so much more.

As we can see in the total return chart below (capital gains + dividends), RYN isn’t a low-volatility stock, which is why I have always avoided it. If I want a volatile stock, I prefer buying a housing supplier that tends to rise faster during times of strong economic growth.

The good news is that the RYN risk/reward looks highly attractive.

As we can see below, despite the increase in the ISM Manufacturing Index (the black line), the year-over-year performance of RYN has continued to go down. At this point, I like the risk/reward a lot.

If we get broadening economic growth, we’ll see both pricing and demand tailwinds in this space. Moreover, while the dividend was cut by 4.6% in February, it was part of the merger. Currently, it yields 5.2% based on a quarterly dividend of $0.27.

At points like these, I think RYN makes for a great investment that provides income, a great risk/reward, as well as inflation protection for an income portfolio, as this stock tends to do well in times of rising inflation.

Generally speaking, I truly believe that all three of these picks bring something truly unique to the table that I find highly compelling for an income portfolio. And, as I always say, stay tuned for more ideas!

For now, here’s a short takeaway:

Takeaway

My introduction was pretty chaotic today. However, my point is that it’s all about identifying capital rotations and finding the best risk/reward for an income portfolio, or any portfolio, really.

At a time when money market funds are overflowing with capital, rates are likely to be pressured, and economic growth is set to rebound, I like to apply a diversified approach to buying high-quality income.

If I were retired today, I would buy ARCC for elevated BDC income, preferred Agree Realty stock due to a 6% yield and a terrific risk/reward, and Rayonier because it provides 5% income and cyclical tailwinds and elevated inflation protection.

These are three different themes, but all have one thing in common, which is a unique ability to add value to an income portfolio.

Three Risks You Need To Know

  • The biggest risk for ARCC is credit risk. While it is protected against further declines in short-term rates, a domino effect in credit could lead to elevated non-accrual rates.
  • For Agree Realty’s preferred stock, the biggest risk is a surge in long-term government debt rates. As these compete with preferred stock due to the bond-like dividend payments, they could keep a lid on capital gains.
  • For RYN, the biggest risk is a sluggish housing market and related pricing headwinds in lumber.

data.title: REITs May Be The Biggest Winner Of The Coming Market Shift
data.description:
Over the past years, we have had many different narratives dominate market headlines and influence the valuations of various sectors.

First, we had the pandemic, which massively favored tech stocks but hurt real-world industries like real estate and entertainment.

Then, we had the historic surge in inflation and interest rates, which again favored tech stocks (QQQ; SPY) and hurt more traditional yield-oriented sectors like MLPs (AMLP), REITs (VNQ), Utilities (XLU), etc.

But I think that the tables are finally turning as we are now in for a new, even more powerful narrative that will reshape the markets over the coming years.

I think that most equities will suffer, even as REITs will emerge as the next big winner, and if you give me 5 minutes of your time, I will explain to you why that is.

AI is the new hot topic, and rightfully so. While many investors seem to think that "AI is overhyped", I still think that it is the opposite. I believe that it is the most disruptive technological innovation of the century, and yet, 84% of people have yet to even use it:

I predicted almost a year ago that as AI models get better over time and more people start using them, it would eventually disrupt lots of businesses, and the market is now finally starting to recognize this risk.

It started with SaaS (Software as a Service) companies (CRM; CNSWF), which have now crashed by 30-50%, almost entirely due to AI breaking barriers to entry, and making it far easier than ever before for new companies to build cheaper alternatives and compete in this space:

But that was just the beginning.

The market is now slowly realizing that SaaS companies are just the tip of the iceberg.

I have previously argued that AI will hurt most businesses and could lead to a broader stock market crash, and I still believe that.

Professional service providers are next in line. Think of consulting companies, marketing agencies, law firms, real estate brokers, engineering firms, etc. Lots of such businesses are publicly listed, and they are getting disrupted as AI makes intelligence far cheaper and broadly available. The world's biggest real estate consulting firm, CBRE (CBRE), just crashed for that exact reason, despite posting strong quarterly results:

And just in case you don't believe me, these are the headlines on Google: It is all about how AI is set to disrupt its business.

Next are all transportation-related businesses, and there are lots of them. Think of trucking companies, logistics firms, delivery, taxis, buses, car manufacturers, gas stations, convenience stores, car insurance companies, lenders financing cars, parking, etc. They will suffer as electric autonomous vehicles greatly reduce the cost of transportation and reduce the need to own a personal car. Lots of major car manufacturers have already suffered from BYD (BYDDF) and Tesla (TSLA) disrupting the car industry, but these are still early days:

Traditional manufacturing businesses will also face severe disruption as AI, robotics, and eventually humanoids disrupt the landscape. Some will likely win big, even as those companies that are too slow to adapt lose big.

Healthcare will also change from top to bottom, as AI can now already answer lots of health-related questions, analyze scans, and even perform surgeries with great precision. Some of this tech is not yet deployed, but it will be over time, and the impact on healthcare providers will be significant.

Office landlords like SL Green (SLG) will also suffer as AI eliminates lots of white-collar jobs, likely leading to higher vacancy rates, lower rents, and more difficulties refinancing these buildings. This is particularly true as tenants will become reluctant to sign new leases if they don't know how many employees they will need in just a few years from now...

If what I said earlier about SaaS businesses, professional service providers, logistics firms, manufacturers, healthcare, and offices is true, then lots of lenders like BDCs (BIZD) and asset managers (GPZ) will also face severe pain as they hold large investments in these sectors. This is already reflected in their share prices, which have crashed due to these fears in the past months:

My point here is that most businesses are likely to suffer over time from AI.

Those that are truly immune to it are the exception. Even things that may seem well-protected, such as a plumbing company, could suffer over time, as more people decide to go into "AI-safe" trades, leading to oversupply and more competition. At the same time, the demand for these services may decline as DIY self-fixes become the norm for smaller issues, thanks to the assistance of AI. Finally, starting a tiny solo plumbing company is now far easier than ever before, with AI handling all your marketing, ads, website design, accounting, customer service, etc. More supply, less demand, lower profits.

So my point here is that very few businesses are truly safe from the deflationary forces of AI.

SaaS businesses were just the first domino, and as the market realizes this, I would expect a lot more stocks to drop to materially lower levels as investors reevaluate their models, reducing long-term growth rates and increasing their discount rates to account for this uncertainty.

As people then sell a lot of these stocks, I expect a historic capital rotation to occur towards AI-resistant asset classes, and I cannot think of anything better than REITs in this context.

Real estate investment trusts, or REITs (VNQ) in short, are publicly listed real estate investment vehicles that primarily own recession- and AI-resistant property types like:

  • Data centers: Digital Realty (DLR)
  • Cell towers: American Tower (AMT)
  • Skilled nursing facilities (OHI)
  • Senior housing (WELL)
  • Hospitals (MPT)
  • Net lease properties (O)
  • Grocery stores (REG)
  • Timberland (WY)
  • Farmland (FPI)
  • Apartment communities (AVB)
  • Single-family homes (AMH)
  • Manufactured housing communities (ELS)
  • E-commerce warehouses (PLD)
  • Distribution centers (PLD)
  • Cold storage (COLD)
  • Self-storage (PSA)
  • Etc.

These real assets are absolutely essential to the survival and prosperity of the human race, and yet, they are strictly limited in supply in their locations, building permits, construction costs, and the availability and cost of financing.

And AI cannot change that.

It cannot remove the need to have a roof over your head or the need to store goods in a warehouse to protect them from the elements.

It also cannot just magically create more land, rewrite zoning, grant new building permits in already built-out desirable locations, provide construction materials, or finance new development projects.

These barriers will always remain and protect existing buildings from new competitors, especially when located in built-out locations with growing demand.

Therefore, REITs are the ultimate anti-AI asset class. They are the exact opposite of anything "artificial" in that they own "real" assets that cannot be easily disrupted.

And as more investors come to this same realization, I would expect a lot more capital to flow towards anti-AI asset classes like REITs, potentially pushing their share prices to a lot higher levels.

You don't have to take it just from me.

This is already happening: REITs clearly benefited from capital flowing from AI-disrupted businesses like SaaS companies into them in early 2026 as they surged in value even as large pockets of the market collapsed:

My prediction is simply that this is still just the beginning, and as AI models only become better and start to disrupt other industries, we will see capital rotate from AI-disrupted to AI-resilient sectors on a much larger scale, turning REITs into the biggest winners of this next market shift.

We are betting heavily on that at High Yield Landlord and expect our portfolio to surge in value over the coming years as this prediction comes to life.
